_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NICE CKS Seborrhoeic Dermatitis, AAD guidelines, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Seborrheic Dermatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Seborrheic dermatitis is a common, chronic, inflammatory skin condition affecting areas rich in sebaceous glands — the scalp, face (nasolabial folds, eyebrows, ears), chest, and back. The condition is characterized by well-demarcated erythematous patches with greasy, yellowish scaling, driven by a combination of sebaceous gland activity, colonization by the yeast Malassezia (formerly Pityrosporum), and individual immune responsiveness. Seborrheic dermatitis affects approximately 3-5% of the global adult population, with peak prevalence in young adults and in individuals over 50. The condition causes significant cosmetic distress due to visible scaling and redness, and can impair quality of life. While not curable, it is highly controllable with antifungal shampoos, topical corticosteroids, and calcineurin inhibitors. In infants, the condition (called cradle cap) is benign and typically self-resolves within the first year of life.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 3-5% of the global adult population; much more common in immunocompetent adults than often appreciated
keyStats.demographics: Peak prevalence in young adults (18-40) and in adults over 50; men are more frequently and more severely affected; more common in dry, cold climates
keyStats.avgAge: Can present at any age — from infancy (cradle cap, benign) to old age; adult peak 30-50; infants typically present in first 3 months

definition: Seborrheic dermatitis (ICD-10: L21) is a chronic, relapsing, inflammatory dermatitis localized to areas with high sebaceous gland density. The pathophysiology involves: (1) sebaceous gland hyperactivity providing lipids that support Malassezia yeast growth; (2) Malassezia species (particularly M. globosa, M. restricta, M. furfur) colonization that triggers inflammatory and immune responses; (3) individual host factors including skin barrier dysfunction, innate immune responsiveness, and possibly neurological factors. The inflammatory response is mediated by Th1 and Th17 cytokines (IL-2, IFN-gamma, IL-17), producing the characteristic erythema and scaling. Malassezia are commensal organisms present in all humans — their pathogenic role in seborrheic dermatitis depends on individual host factors rather than simple infection. The condition is managed by dermatologists and general practitioners.

typesClassification.description: Presents in the first 3 months of life with greasy, yellowish scaling on the scalp (most common), face, and intertriginous areas. Non-pruritic and benign. Believed related to transplacental maternal hormone effects on sebaceous glands. Self-resolves within months to 1 year in most cases.

The most common presentation in adults. Ranges from mild, dry scaling (dandruff) to diffuse, greasy scaling with underlying erythema involving the entire scalp. May extend to the hairline, forehead, and posterior neck. Pruritus is variable.

typesClassification.type: Adult Seborrheic Dermatitis — Face and Body
typesClassification.description: Erythematous, well-demarcated patches with greasy, yellowish scaling on the nasolabial folds, paranasal areas, eyebrows, glabella, ears (postauricular area), central chest, and upper back. The lesions follow the distribution of sebaceous glands. Moderate pruritus is common.

In HIV/AIDS patients, organ transplant recipients, and those with neurological disease (Parkinson's, stroke), seborrheic dermatitis is more severe, extensive, and refractory to treatment. In HIV, it may be the first sign of immunodeficiency and correlates with low CD4 counts.

primarySymptoms: Greasy, yellowish scaling on erythematous base — the hallmark presentation. The scaling is characteristically greasy (not dry and powdery) and yellow-white in color, on a background of diffuse or patchy redness. Most prominent on the scalp, nasolabial folds, and eyebrows.
Scalp scaling ranging from fine to thick — from mild dandruff (fine, dry, white scaling) to thick, greasy, adherent plaques covering the entire scalp. Scaling may be diffuse or in patches.

Facial erythema and scaling — well-demarcated erythematous patches with greasy scaling in the nasolabial folds, paranasal areas, glabella, eyebrows, and postauricular folds. This distribution is characteristic and distinguishes seborrheic dermatitis from other facial dermatoses.
Pruritus — variable but often present, particularly on the scalp. In severe disease, scratching can cause secondary excoriations and infection.
Ear involvement — scaling and erythema in the external ear canal, behind the ears (postauricular area), and on the earlobes. Ear involvement often accompanies facial seborrheic dermatitis.
Chest and back involvement — in moderate-to-severe disease, lesions extend to the central chest and upper back where sebaceous glands are concentrated.
In infants: thick, greasy, yellow scaling on scalp — 'cradle cap' with thick, greasy plaques that may extend to the forehead and eyebrows. Non-pruritic and benign.
earlyWarningSigns: Greasy, yellowish scaling in the nasolabial folds, eyebrows, or scalp — the characteristic appearance of seborrheic dermatitis
Flaking scalp that responds to dandruff shampoo — mild seborrheic dermatitis often presents as dandruff unresponsive to regular shampoo
Facial redness and scaling that worsens in dry, cold weather — seborrheic dermatitis often worsens in winter
Scaly eyebrows and ear involvement — involvement of eyebrows and ears in addition to the nasolabial folds is characteristic
Recurrent scaling in the same areas — the chronic, relapsing nature of the condition with recurrence in the same distribution
emergencySigns: Signs of secondary infection — extensive erythema, weeping, crusting, and warmth suggesting impetigo or cellulitis requires urgent evaluation
Seborrheic dermatitis with extensive weeping and edema in infants — consider erythroderma or other severe inflammatory skin conditions
Severe, intractable seborrheic dermatitis in an adult — consider HIV infection or other immunodeficiency; seborrheic dermatitis as the presenting sign of AIDS is documented
Facial seborrheic dermatitis with severe eye involvement — periorbital scaling and irritation requires evaluation to rule out ocular complications

causes.cause: Malassezia yeast colonization and immune response
causes.description: Malassezia species (lipophilic yeasts that are normal skin commensals) are central to seborrheic dermatitis pathogenesis. The yeast produces lipase enzymes that release free fatty acids from sebaceous triglycerides, which trigger inflammatory and irritant responses in susceptible individuals. Different Malassezia species (M. globosa, M. restricta, M. furfur) predominate in different populations. Individual immune responsiveness to Malassezia determines whether colonization progresses to disease.

Seborrheic dermatitis occurs in areas with high sebaceous gland density — the scalp, face, and upper trunk. Conditions that increase sebaceous gland activity (androgen levels, hormonal changes, heat, humidity) worsen seborrheic dermatitis. However, the condition also occurs in individuals with normal sebum levels, indicating that sebaceous gland activity alone is insufficient.

Compromised skin barrier function (reduced ceramides, impaired stratum corneum) allows increased penetration of irritants, allergens, and microbial products. Patients with seborrheic dermatitis show elevated transepidermal water loss and reduced skin hydration, particularly in affected areas.

Seborrheic dermatitis is significantly more common and severe in patients with neurological conditions including Parkinson's disease (5-7x increased prevalence), facial nerve palsy, and after stroke. The mechanism involves altered skin innervation, sebaceous gland activity, and potentially reduced grooming habits. These associations support a role for neurogenic factors in disease pathogenesis.

The inflammatory response in seborrheic dermatitis involves a Th1 and Th17 skewed immune response, with elevated IL-2, IFN-gamma, IL-6, and IL-17. Individuals with HIV/AIDS (CD4 <200) have severe, widespread seborrheic dermatitis, indicating that immune competence normally keeps the condition in check.

Cold, dry weather worsens seborrheic dermatitis (explaining the winter exacerbation seen in many patients). Heat and humidity can worsen intertriginous involvement. Stress, fatigue, and anxiety are reported triggers for flares. Alcohol use and neurological disease are associated with more severe disease.

riskFactors.description: Men are more frequently and more severely affected by seborrheic dermatitis than women. This is related to androgen-driven increases in sebaceous gland activity and possibly differences in immune responsiveness.

The condition is uncommon before puberty and peaks in young adults (20-40) and again after age 50. In older adults, decreased immune competence and possibly reduced skin repair capacity contribute to increased prevalence.

riskFactors.factor: Neurological conditions (Parkinson's disease, stroke, facial nerve palsy)
riskFactors.description: Parkinson's disease carries a 5-7x increased risk of seborrheic dermatitis, likely related to altered sebaceous gland activity and skin innervation. Facial nerve palsy and stroke also increase risk, possibly through reduced skin care and altered innervation.

HIV/AIDS patients have a significantly higher prevalence and more severe seborrheic dermatitis. In untreated AIDS, seborrheic dermatitis may be extensive, severe, and refractory. It can be an early marker of immunosuppression. Post-transplant immunosuppression also increases risk.

Seborrheic dermatitis typically worsens in winter in temperate climates, similar to atopic dermatitis. Cold, dry air damages the skin barrier and may alter Malassezia ecology. Indoor heating further reduces humidity.

Emotional stress, anxiety, and physical fatigue are reported precipitants of flares in many patients. Stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and may alter immune function, exacerbating inflammatory skin conditions.

Infrequent washing allows scaling to accumulate. Both excessive washing (stripping skin barrier) and inadequate washing (allowing scaling buildup) can worsen seborrheic dermatitis. Optimal frequency is individual.

affectedDemographics: Seborrheic dermatitis affects approximately 3-5% of the global adult population — an estimated 200-300 million people worldwide
Men are more frequently and more severely affected than women (approximately 3:1 male-to-female ratio)
The condition is rare in preadolescent children, begins to appear in adolescence, and peaks in young adults (20-40 years) and again in adults over 50
In infants, seborrheic dermatitis (cradle cap) is extremely common — affecting 10-60% of newborns in the first 3 months of life; benign and self-resolving
Patients with Parkinson's disease have a 5-7x increased risk; patients with HIV/AIDS (CD4 <200) have more severe and extensive disease in up to 30-40%
Prevalence is higher in cold, dry climates and lower in warm, humid environments — environmental factors influence disease severity
diagnosisOverview: Seborrheic dermatitis diagnosis is clinical, based on the characteristic distribution (sebaceous gland-bearing areas: scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, ears, central chest), appearance (well-demarcated erythema with greasy, yellowish scaling), and chronic, relapsing course. The differential diagnosis includes: scalp psoriasis (silvery scales, extension beyond hairline, nail pitting), atopic dermatitis (flexural involvement, personal/family atopic history, more pruritic), tinea capitis (scaly patches with hair loss, more common in children), contact dermatitis (history of contact exposure, distribution matches contact pattern), and rosacea (erythema of central face without scaling). In HIV/AIDS, atypical presentations (more extensive, more severe) may require biopsy to rule out other dermatoses. For typical presentations in immunocompetent adults, no laboratory testing is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone of diagnosis. Characteristic appearance in typical distribution (scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, ears, chest) establishes the diagnosis. History of chronic, relapsing course supports the diagnosis. No laboratory testing required for typical presentations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination of scalp, face, chest, and back for erythema and scaling. The clinician will assess the distribution and quality of scaling (greasy, yellowish vs dry, silvery).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Primarily to exclude tinea capitis (which may fluoresce under Wood lamp) and to assess for hypopigmentation or other concurrent conditions. Not diagnostic for seborrheic dermatitis itself.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Handheld Wood lamp examination in a darkened room; may show yellow-green fluorescence in some Malassezia species but this is not diagnostic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: To exclude tinea capitis and other fungal infections when scaling is extensive or hair loss is present. KOH prep will show fungal hyphae in tinea; Malassezia yeast forms (spores and short hyphae) may be seen in seborrheic dermatitis but are not diagnostic.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Superficial skin scraping examined under microscope after KOH digestion. Painless, takes minutes. Ordered when fungal infection is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In patients with severe, extensive, or treatment-refractory seborrheic dermatitis, HIV testing should be considered given that seborrheic dermatitis can be an early marker of immunosuppression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test for HIV antibodies and p24 antigen; results within 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for seborrheic dermatitis is directed at controlling symptoms rather than curing the underlying condition — the condition is chronic and relapsing. For the scalp: regular use of antifungal shampoos (ketoconazole 2%, selenium sulfide 2.5%, zinc pyrithione 1-2%, ciclopirox 1%) is first-line. These reduce Malassezia colonization and control scaling. For moderate-to-severe scalp disease, topical corticosteroids (fluocinolone oil, clobetasol shampoo) provide rapid control. For facial and body lesions: topical azoles (ketoconazole cream, Sertaconazole) and calcineurin inhibitors (pimecrolimus, tacrolimus) are first-line — they are anti-inflammatory and antifungal without steroid side effects. Low-potency topical steroids (hydrocortisone 1%) are used short-term for flares. In infants, frequent shampooing and gentle scale removal with a soft brush is sufficient; severe cases may require low-potency topical steroids. Lifelong maintenance therapy (antifungal shampoo 1-2x weekly) is typically needed to prevent relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: Antifungal shampoos (Ketoconazole 2%, Selenium sulfide 2.5%, Zinc pyrithione 1-2%, Ciclopirox 1%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for scalp seborrheic dermatitis. Antifungal agents reduce Malassezia colonization, which drives inflammation. Used 2-3 times weekly, leaving shampoo on scalp for 3-5 minutes before rinsing. Rotating between two different antifungal agents reduces the development of resistance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Highly effective for scalp scaling and erythema — 60-80% improvement with regular use. May take 2-4 weeks for full effect. Maintenance 1-2x weekly prevents relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids — Fluocinolone acetonide 0.01% oil (scalp), Clobetasol 0.05% shampoo/foam (scalp), Hydrocortisone 1% cream (face/body)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapidly effective anti-inflammatory agents for controlling flares. For scalp: fluocinolone oil or clobetasol shampoo/foam; for face/body: hydrocortisone 1% cream. Use short-term (2-4 weeks) to control flares; prolonged use causes skin atrophy and other steroid side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid and effective — symptoms improve within days. Should be used short-term only due to side effects (skin thinning, telangiectasias with prolonged face/body use; scalp folliculitis with prolonged scalp use).

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors — Pimecrolimus 1% cream, Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment
medicalTreatments.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory creams that inhibit T-cell activation and cytokine production. Particularly useful for facial seborrheic dermatitis where steroid side effects are a concern. Apply twice daily to affected areas.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for facial and body seborrheic dermatitis — comparable to low-potency steroids without skin atrophy risk. Slight burning on application that resolves with use. Does not cause steroid side effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical azoles — Ketoconazole 2% cream, Sertaconazole 2% cream
medicalTreatments.description: Topical antifungal agents with anti-inflammatory properties. Ketoconazole cream applied twice daily to affected facial areas is effective. Also comes in cream form for facial/body application.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for mild-to-moderate facial seborrheic dermatitis. 50-70% improvement in 4-6 weeks. Well-tolerated. Can be combined with low-potency topical steroid for faster control.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole 0.75% gel or cream
medicalTreatments.description: A topical agent with anti-inflammatory properties effective in rosacea and also used for seborrheic dermatitis. Applied twice daily to affected facial areas.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for mild-to-moderate seborrheic dermatitis; 50-60% improvement in 4-6 weeks. Particularly useful when seborrheic dermatitis co-exists with rosacea.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-potency topical corticosteroids for infants (Hydrocortisone 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: For infant seborrheic dermatitis that is moderate or unresponsive to gentle measures alone, low-potency hydrocortisone 1% cream applied twice daily for 1-2 weeks is safe and effective. The condition is benign and self-resolving.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective and safe in infants when used short-term. Should be accompanied by gentle scale removal and regular shampooing.

surgicalOptions.name: There are no surgical options for seborrheic dermatitis
surgicalOptions.description: Seborrheic dermatitis is a medical condition managed with topical therapy; surgery is not indicated.

alternativeTreatments.name: Frequent shampooing and scale removal
alternativeTreatments.description: For scalp seborrheic dermatitis, frequent shampooing (daily or every other day) reduces scaling buildup and improves appearance. Gentle scale removal with a soft brush or comb during shampooing helps.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Supportive measure that improves scalp appearance. Particularly useful for mild disease. More effective when combined with antifungal shampoo.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea tree oil shampoo
alternativeTreatments.description: Tea tree oil has antifungal and anti-inflammatory properties. 5% tea tree oil shampoo has shown efficacy in seborrheic dermatitis in small RCTs. Available over-the-counter.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest efficacy in mild-to-moderate scalp seborrheic dermatitis. May be less effective than ketoconazole shampoo but is a reasonable natural alternative.

alternativeTreatments.name: Stress management
alternativeTreatments.description: Stress is a reported trigger for seborrheic dermatitis flares. Stress reduction techniques (mindfulness, meditation, exercise, therapy) may reduce flare frequency.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Does not replace topical therapy but may reduce flare frequency in patients for whom stress is a trigger.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be seen when over-the-counter antifungal shampoos have not controlled symptoms; when facial seborrheic dermatitis requires prescription topical therapy; when the diagnosis is uncertain (to distinguish from psoriasis, rosacea, or contact dermatitis); when the condition is causing significant cosmetic distress or quality of life impact; and when treatment-refractory or atypical presentations suggest an underlying immunodeficiency (HIV). General practitioners can manage mild, typical seborrheic dermatitis with antifungal shampoos and topical steroids.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist — most can effectively manage seborrheic dermatitis. For treatment-refractory cases or atypical presentations, a dermatologist with interest in inflammatory skin conditions is preferable. The condition is straightforward to manage and does not typically require highly specialized care unless it is extensive, refractory, or associated with underlying immunodeficiency.
preventionStrategies: Maintain regular antifungal shampoo use — 1-2x weekly after the condition is controlled prevents relapse
Avoid triggers — cold, dry weather worsens the condition; use a humidifier in heated indoor spaces during winter
Manage stress — stress is a reported trigger for flares; stress reduction techniques may reduce flare frequency
Maintain good skincare — gentle, regular cleansing of affected areas without over-washing (which can strip skin barrier)
Avoid heavy, occlusive products on the face — thick moisturizers and products containing petrolatum can worsen facial seborrheic dermatitis
lifestyleModifications: Use antifungal shampoo 1-2x weekly as maintenance — even when the condition is controlled, regular use prevents relapse
Apply topical medications to affected areas as directed — consistency is critical for control
Use a gentle, non-soap cleanser for facial and body skin — avoid harsh soaps that strip the skin barrier
Avoid heavy, occlusive facial products — use light, non-comedogenic moisturizers; avoid products containing petrolatum on the face
Keep a symptom diary — to identify personal triggers (stress, weather, certain products) and optimize management
Use a humidifier in dry, heated indoor environments — adds moisture to indoor air and reduces winter flares

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet — omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed), fruits, vegetables, and whole grains may reduce systemic inflammation
Zinc-rich foods — zinc has anti-inflammatory and skin-repair properties; oysters, pumpkin seeds, beans, and lean meats
Vitamin D supplementation — vitamin D has immunomodulatory effects; deficiency is common in northern latitudes and may worsen inflammatory skin conditions
Biotin and B vitamin supplementation — supports skin health; deficiency can manifest as dermatitis
avoid: High-sugar, processed food diet — pro-inflammatory diet may worsen systemic inflammation and skin conditions
Excessive alcohol — alcohol may worsen seborrheic dermatitis through immunosuppression and inflammatory mechanisms
Dairy (individual variation) — some patients report improvement with dairy elimination; evidence is anecdotal but a trial is reasonable
Foods high in saturated and trans fats — pro-inflammatory fats may contribute to systemic inflammation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is beneficial and recommended for seborrheic dermatitis patients. Exercise reduces stress (a potential trigger), supports immune function, and improves overall health. No specific restrictions apply — normal exercise is safe. However, sweating can worsen facial and intertriginous seborrheic dermatitis — shower and change promptly after exercise, cleanse affected areas, and apply any maintenance topical medications. Swimming is an excellent option but pool chlorine may be drying — shower and apply moisturizer after swimming.
dailyManagement: Use antifungal shampoo 2-3x weekly — leave on scalp for 3-5 minutes, then rinse; alternate between two different antifungal agents
Apply topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors to face as prescribed — for facial flares, apply hydrocortisone 1% or pimecrolimus/tacrolimus as directed
Use antifungal cream (ketoconazole 2%) on facial lesions if prescribed — apply twice daily to affected areas
Maintain facial hygiene with gentle, non-soap cleanser — avoid harsh soaps and abrasive scrubs
Apply maintenance topical therapy 1-2x weekly — even when controlled, regular use of antifungal shampoo prevents relapse
Monitor for triggers — note weather changes, stress, and product changes that precede flares
prognosis: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing condition with no cure. However, it is highly controllable with appropriate topical therapy. With regular antifungal shampoo use and intermittent topical steroids or calcineurin inhibitors, most patients achieve and maintain good control. The condition has a benign course in immunocompetent individuals — it is uncomfortable and cosmetically distressing but not dangerous. In infants, the condition (cradle cap) typically resolves spontaneously by 6-12 months of age without lasting effects. In adults, lifelong maintenance therapy is typically required to prevent relapse. Factors that worsen prognosis include: immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS), neurological disease, and inadequate maintenance therapy.
recoveryTimeline: With antifungal shampoo alone (mild disease): improvement at 2-4 weeks, optimal at 6-8 weeks. With topical corticosteroid (moderate flares): improvement within days, controlled at 2-4 weeks. With combination therapy (shampoo + topical agents): fastest control, typically 2-4 weeks for significant improvement. Maintenance therapy (antifungal shampoo 1-2x weekly): prevents relapse indefinitely. In infants: cradle cap typically resolves by 6-12 months with gentle measures.

complications: Secondary infection — scratching can introduce bacteria causing impetigo or cellulitis; weeping, crusting, and warmth indicate infection requiring topical or oral antibiotics
Seborrheic dermatitis of the external ear canal — can cause itching, scaling, and secondary bacterial or fungal infection of the ear canal (otomycosis)
Blepharitis — scaling on the eyelids can cause secondary blepharitis; requires eyelid hygiene and sometimes ophthalmology referral
Psychosocial impact — visible facial redness and scaling causes embarrassment and reduced quality of life in some patients
Contact dermatitis from treatments — patients can develop contact allergy to topical medications (particularly ketoconazole, topical steroids), complicating management
Perioral dermatitis-like eruption — aggressive use of topical corticosteroids on the face can trigger perioral dermatitis; use steroids sparingly and short-term on the face

supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the largest dermatologic professional organization.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS: Seborrhoeic Dermatitis. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2023.
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary with evidence-based management recommendations.

supportResources.name: National Eczema Association — Seborrheic Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Patient education resources on seborrheic dermatitis from the National Eczema Association.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scalp psoriasis shows silvery, dry scales (not greasy) and typically extends beyond the hairline onto the forehead; nail pitting is common. Seborrheic dermatitis has greasy, yellowish scales limited to the scalp. Both can co-exist (sebopsoriasis).

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea causes erythema and flushing on the central face but lacks the greasy scaling of seborrheic dermatitis. Rosacea typically spares the nasolabial folds; seborrheic dermatitis characteristically involves them. Both can co-exist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Contact dermatitis shows a distribution pattern that corresponds to the contact exposure (cosmetic, skincare product, topical medication). Seborrheic dermatitis follows sebaceous gland distribution regardless of product use. History of contact exposure is key.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea Capitis (Scalp Ringworm)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea capitis shows scaly patches with hair loss, broken hairs, and often cervical lymphadenopathy; KOH prep or fungal culture is positive. Seborrheic dermatitis does not cause hair loss. Tinea is more common in children.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis (Sebopsoriasis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Sebopsoriasis is the overlap between seborrheic dermatitis and psoriasis, presenting with features of both conditions. It is common and responds to treatments for both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Cradle Cap (Infantile Seborrheic Dermatitis)
relatedConditions.relevance: The infantile form of seborrheic dermatitis, called cradle cap. Benign, self-resolving, and managed with gentle measures. May be the first sign of atopic dermatitis in some infants.

faqs.question: What is the difference between seborrheic dermatitis and dandruff?
faqs.answer: Dandruff and seborrheic dermatitis exist on a spectrum of the same condition. Mild seborrheic dermatitis of the scalp presents as simple dandruff — dry, fine, white scaling without significant erythema. More severe seborrheic dermatitis adds erythema and greasy, yellowish scaling to the picture. Both respond to antifungal shampoos. The difference is one of severity, not of kind.

faqs.question: What causes seborrheic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Seborrheic dermatitis is caused by a combination of three factors: (1) sebaceous gland activity providing lipids that support Malassezia yeast growth; (2) Malassezia yeast colonization that triggers inflammatory and immune responses; and (3) individual host factors (skin barrier function, immune responsiveness) that determine whether colonization progresses to disease. It is not caused by poor hygiene (though infrequent washing allows scaling to accumulate) and is not contagious.

faqs.question: Is seborrheic dermatitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No — seborrheic dermatitis is absolutely not contagious. Malassezia yeast are normal skin commensals present in everyone. The condition results from individual host factors (immune response, skin barrier, sebaceous gland activity) determining how the skin reacts to these normal organisms. It cannot be transmitted through contact.

faqs.question: How is seborrheic dermatitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on severity and location. For scalp disease: antifungal shampoos (ketoconazole, zinc pyrithione, selenium sulfide, ciclopirox) are first-line — use 2-3x weekly. For flares: topical corticosteroids (fluocinolone oil, clobetasol shampoo) provide rapid control. For facial disease: topical azoles (ketoconazole cream), calcineurin inhibitors (pimecrolimus, tacrolimus), or low-potency steroids (hydrocortisone 1%) short-term. Maintenance antifungal shampoo use prevents relapse.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for seborrheic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for seborrheic dermatitis — it is a chronic, relapsing condition. However, it is highly controllable with appropriate treatment. Regular use of antifungal shampoo and intermittent use of topical steroids or calcineurin inhibitors controls the condition in the vast majority of patients. Lifelong maintenance therapy is typically required to prevent relapse.

faqs.question: What is cradle cap and how is it treated?
faqs.answer: Cradle cap is infantile seborrheic dermatitis — thick, greasy, yellowish scaling on the scalp of newborns and young infants. It is benign, non-pruritic, and typically self-resolves by 6-12 months of age. Treatment is gentle: frequent shampooing, gentle scale removal with a soft brush, and in more severe cases, low-potency topical hydrocortisone 1% cream for 1-2 weeks. It does not cause any lasting effects and does not require aggressive treatment.

faqs.question: Does diet affect seborrheic dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Dietary effects on seborrheic dermatitis are not well-established. An anti-inflammatory diet (omega-3 fatty acids, fruits and vegetables, whole grains) may support overall skin health. Some patients report improvement with dairy elimination, but evidence is anecdotal. There is no specific diet proven to cure or significantly improve seborrheic dermatitis.

faqs.question: Can seborrheic dermatitis be a sign of something more serious?
faqs.answer: In immunocompetent adults, seborrheic dermatitis is benign — it is uncomfortable and cosmetically distressing but not dangerous. However, severe, extensive, or treatment-refractory seborrheic dermatitis in adults can be a sign of immunosuppression, particularly HIV/AIDS. If your seborrheic dermatitis is severe, widespread, and not responding to standard treatment, discuss HIV testing with your doctor. Neurological conditions (Parkinson's disease) also increase seborrheic dermatitis severity.

metaTitle: Seborrheic Dermatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Treatment & Cradle Cap | aihealz
metaDescription: Seborrheic dermatitis: greasy, yellowish scaling on scalp, face, and chest. Learn about causes (Malassezia yeast), antifungal shampoos, topical treatments, and difference from dandruff.

images.altText: Clinical presentation of seborrheic dermatitis showing erythematous patches with greasy, yellowish scaling on the face and nasolabial folds
images.caption: Seborrheic dermatitis characteristically affects the nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and central face with erythema and greasy scaling.

images.altText: Infant with cradle cap showing thick, greasy, yellowish scaling on the scalp
images.caption: Cradle cap (infantile seborrheic dermatitis) is benign and typically resolves by 6-12 months of age.

images.altText: Diagram showing typical distribution of seborrheic dermatitis affecting scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, ears, and central chest
images.caption: Seborrheic dermatitis affects areas with high sebaceous gland density — scalp, face, and upper chest.
